title: Rapid Atmospheric Vapor Deposition of H:In2O3 Transparent Conducting Oxide Thin Films
authors: Xiaoyu Guo, Hae-Jun Seok, Eilidh L. Quinn, Matthew K Sharpe, Callum. D. McAleese, Yi-Teng Huang, Xinjuan Li, Kexue Li, Chia-Yu Chang, Yongjie Wang, John O'Sullivan, Katie L. Moore, Caterina Ducati, Ruy Sebastian Bonilla, Han-Ki Kim, Abderrahime Sekkat, Robert L. Z. Hoye
abstract: Transparent conducting oxides (TCOs) are essential for the optoelectronics industry, but there is a critical gap in cost-effective methods to rapidly deposit low sheet resistance, high transmittance films without damaging delicate materials, including emerging soft semiconductors like metal-halide perovskites. In this work, atmospheric pressure chemical vapor deposition (AP-CVD) is used to synthesise H:In2O3 films with 7.20+/-0.01 Ohm/sq sheet resistance (0.50+/-0.06 mOhm.cm resistivity) and transmittance up to 89% in the near-infrared (NIR), surpassing commercial sputter-deposited indium tin oxide. The growth rate is 40x higher than atomic layer deposition (ALD), and the AP-CVD films are fully processed under atmospheric conditions at only 140 C. Comparison of secondary ion mass spectrometry and time-of-flight elastic recoil detection analysis with changes in carrier concentration indicate that H dopants are introduced from the water oxidant. There is an increase in mobility form 40+/-10 cm2/Vs to 160+/-30 cm2/Vs when changing from O2 to H2O as the oxidant, which is attributed to H dopants passivating oxygen vacancies that act as carrier scattering centers. This work establishes AP-CVD as a promising method for manufacturing high figure-of-merit TCOs in a rapid, scalable and cost-effective manner, using mild growth conditions compatible with thermally-sensitive materials.
summary: This work introduces Atmospheric Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (AP-CVD) as a highly scalable and low-temperature method for creating high-performance Transparent Conducting Oxides ($	ext{H:In}_2	ext{O}_3$). By using water as an oxidant, the researchers significantly boost carrier mobility and NIR transmittance, outperforming established commercial methods and making TCOs compatible with sensitive electronic substrates.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for cost-effective, rapid, and low-temperature methods to deposit high-performance Transparent Conducting Oxides (TCOs) that can be compatible with sensitive materials like perovskites.
main_result: Atmospheric Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (AP-CVD) successfully synthesizes hydrogen-doped $	ext{In}_2	ext{O}_3$ films that surpass commercial sputter-deposited ITO in performance, achieving high mobility and superior Near-Infrared (NIR) transmittance.
method: The study uses AP-CVD under mild, atmospheric conditions, systematically varying the oxidant chemistry ($	ext{O}_2$ vs. $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$) and employing advanced characterization techniques like ToF-ERDA, XPS, and Nano-SIMS.
model_or_system: The physical system is the deposition of $	ext{H:In}_2	ext{O}_3$ thin films using a close-proximity SALD reactor operating in CVD mode. The process is compared against high-vacuum methods like sputtering and slow methods like ALD.
key_observables: Sheet resistance ($	ext{7.20}\pm0.01 	ext{ } \Omega \square^{-1}$), NIR transmittance ($	ext{up to } 89\%$), carrier mobility ($	ext{up to } 160\pm30 	ext{ cm}^2 	ext{ V}^{-1} 	ext{ s}^{-1}$), and depth-dependent $	ext{D/H}$ ratios.
important_parameters: Low processing temperature ($	ext{140 }^\circ	ext{C}$), high growth rate ($	ext{40x}$ higher than ALD), and the oxidant source ($	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$ vs. $	ext{O}_2$).
main_assumptions: The improved electrical properties are attributed to hydrogen dopants passivating oxygen vacancies, which act as carrier scattering centers.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the AP-CVD setup, comparisons of growth rates and process times against ALD/sputtering, and optical transmittance spectra showing superior NIR performance compared to sputtered ITO.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the limitations of existing TCO deposition methods, presenting the AP-CVD technique, demonstrating superior electrical/optical performance using $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$ oxidants, and finally using advanced spectroscopy (SIMS, ToF-ERDA) to confirm the role of hydrogen doping in enhancing material quality.
why_interesting: While primarily materials science, the control over defect states (oxygen vacancies) via chemical doping ($	ext{H}$ incorporation) and the comparison of different deposition kinetics (CVD vs. ALD) touches upon non-equilibrium thermodynamics and defect physics relevant to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The substrate is loaded onto a platen (metal plate), which heats the sample and oscillates it beneath the gas manifold. b. Growth rate of H:In2O3 thin films deposited by AP-CVD with
Figure 1d and Table 1. Films grown with N2/H2O in the oxidant channel exhibited the lowest resistivity

Figure 2. Effect of oxidant on optical properties, surface chemistry and morphology of AP-CVD H:In2O3 films. a. Transmittance and bandgap of H:In2O3 thin films deposited via AP-CVD at 140 °C for 1500 cycles under various oxidizing environments. The thicknesses of film grown with O2/H2O, N2/H2O, O2 alone and O2/N2 in the oxidant channel are 320±30, 470±40, 500±40, 560±50 nm, respectively. b. Visible-IR transmittance of commercial ITO (150±3 nm thickness) and optimal N2/H2O-grown H:In2O3 thin film (710±50 nm thickness) deposited at 140 °C for 2000 cycles. c. XPS spectra, and d. high-resolution SEM images of H:In2O3 thin films deposited under various oxidizing environments for 1500 cycles.
